content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 27, 1990

Karen Brainerd
Legislative Assistant
Respect Our Animal Rights
915 "L" Street, Suite 1000
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File Nos. I-90-413 and I-90-551

Dear Ms. Brainerd:

You have requested advice on behalf of Respect Our Animals'
Rights ("ROAR") and Sharley Wechsler, a member of ROAR's board of
directors, under the campaign reporting provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1/ Because your request is a
general inquiry, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).2/

QUESTIONS

1. May a person concurrently act as treasurer for an elected
state officer's political action committees and sit on the board
of directors of ROAR, a political action committee formerly
controlled by the same elected state officer? Does such activity
create a presumption of control of ROAR by the elected state
officer?

2. If the above activity is permissible, are there any
restrictions as to the types of decisions in which the treasurer
can participate? For example, may the treasurer participate in
decisions concerning making expenditures to, or on behalf of, the
elected state officer for whom she is employed?

_______________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83113; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File Nos. I-90-413 and I-90-551
Page 2


     3.  Does the additional information contained in this letter
change the advice we gave in the former Brainerd Advice Letter,
No. A-90-039, concerning the status of ROAR as a candidate
controlled committee.

CONCLUSIONS

     1. and 2.  If the treasurer of an elected state officer's
controlled committees is a voting member of another committee's
board of directors, there is a possibility that the elected
officer indirectly controls the committee.  If the treasurer sits
on the other committee's board of directors on behalf of the
elected state officer, the possibility of indirect committee
control becomes a presumption.  Therefore, under the facts
presented in this case, to ensure that ROAR is not characterized
as a committee controlled by the elected state officer, no agent
of the elected state officer or person acting as a representative
of a committee controlled by the state officer may sit on the
board of directors or participate in making decisions concerning
the expenditures of ROAR.

     3.  Soliciting funds on behalf of ROAR, by itself, does not
indicate control of ROAR.  That is why we concluded that Senator
Roberti could engage in such an activity on behalf of ROAR without
creating a presumption of control.  Our conclusion assumed that
his degree of involvement in the committee was so limited.

FACTS

     Senator Roberti founded and controlled an animal rights
political action committee, Respect Our Animals' Rights ("ROAR").
Effective January 1, 1989, Senator Roberti resigned from the
board of directors of ROAR.

     Shirley Wechsler sits on the board of directors of ROAR.
Currently, there are only four members on the board of directors,
of which she is one.  Shirley Wechsler also is the treasurer for
Senator Roberti's political action committees.  She does not act
as a treasurer for any other elected official apart from Senator
Roberti and his political action committees.  Ms. Wechsler has
informed you that she sits on the board of directors of ROAR as a
private citizen and not on behalf of Senator Roberti.

     Initially, your inquiry was whether the (unnamed) treasurer
of political action committees of Senator Roberti may sit on the
board of directors of ROAR, or whether her position as treasurer

Our File Nos. I-90-413 and I-90-551
Page 3


would preclude her from such an activity.3/ Subsequently, you
informed us that Shirley Wechsler was the treasurer, that she
already was sitting on the board of directors of ROAR, and that
she had served in that capacity since the inception of ROAR.

ANALYSIS

As a general rule, a candidate controls a committee whenever
the candidate has a significant influence on the actions or
decisions of the committee. (Section 82016.)

We look at the degree of the candidate's involvement in a
committee's activities to determine whether the candidate controls
the committee. (Madden Advice Letter, No. A-85-197, copy
enclosed.) For that reason, although certain activities of a
candidate or elected official are not sufficient by themselves to
constitute control, each is a factor to be considered in
determining whether the candidate controls the committee. For
example, soliciting funds on behalf of a committee by itself would
not indicate control of the committee. Other illustrations of
activities which are not sufficient by themselves to constitute
control of the committee are as follows: attending fundraising
activities of the committee, allowing the candidate's or elected
official's name on any type of letterhead of the committee, having
members of the candidate's or elected official's controlled
committee actively participate in the affairs of the committee,
or, accepting funds from the committee. However, such activities
are relevant to whether such a committee is controlled by the
candidate or elected official. (Woodruff Advice Letter, No. I-89-
180, copy enclosed.)4/

The Commission has interpreted "controlled committee" very
broadly to include any significant participation in the actions of
a committee by a candidate, his or her agent, or representative of
any other committee he or she controls. Accordingly, we also look

_______________________
3/
Your initial request for advice was dated June 8, 1990. As a
result of my telephone conversation with David Nagler, Executive
Vice President of ROAR, a follow-up letter was sent to us on
July 10, 1990. Additional information was provided us by
telephone on August 21, 1990 and November 5 and 6, 1990.

4/
That is why in our March 5, 1990 letter to you (Brainerd
Advice Letter, No. A-90-039, copy enclosed), we concluded that
Senator Roberti could write a letter soliciting contributions on
behalf of the committee he had founded and formerly controlled
without creating a presumption of control of the committee,
provided that was the only action taken by him. Note that our
advice was premised in part on the fact that he had resigned from
the board of directors of this committee. We did not know at that
time that Senator Roberti's treasurer was sitting on the board of
directors of ROAR or that Senator Roberti's name appeared on the
letterhead of ROAR.

Our File Nos. I-90-413 and I-90-551
Page 4


at the degree of the candidate's agent's or representative's
involvement in the committee's activities to determine whether the
candidate controls the committee. For example, if members of a
candidate's or elected official's own controlled committee are
active members of another political action committee, there is a
possibility that the candidate or elected official indirectly
controls the committee. (Madden, supra.) If, on behalf of the
elected official, members of a candidate's or elected official's
controlled committee are voting members of a committee's board of
directors, a presumption exists that the candidate or elected
official indirectly controls the committee. (Ferguson Advice
Letter, No. A-86-044, copy enclosed.)

In your initial inquiry, you asked whether the treasurer of
Senator Robert's controlled committees could serve as a member of
the board of directors of ROAR. The concern was that her
position as treasurer for Senator Roberti would preclude her from
such an involvement with ROAR. Subsequently we learned the
identity of Senator Roberti's treasurer as Shirley Wechsler, and
the fact she already was sitting on the board of directors of
ROAR. As you are aware, the Commission does not provide advice
relating to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)
Therefore, we can only advise you as to whether it is permissible
for Ms. Wechsler to act as Senator Roberti's treasurer and
continue to sit on the board, and if so, whether there are any
restrictions concerning the types of committee decisions in which
she may and may not participate in the future.

In order to determine whether Shirley Wechsler may continue
to sit on the board of directors of ROAR, we first need to
ascertain whether ROAR is a committee controlled by her employer,
Senator Roberti, or, is, in fact, an independent political action
committee. Looking at the factors to measure a candidate's or
elected official's degree of involvement, we note that Senator
Roberti's name is prominently listed on the ROAR's stationery,
that Senator Roberti solicits funds for ROAR committee, that ROAR
presumably makes expenditures either to, or on behalf of, Senator
Roberti, and that the treasurer of Senator Roberti's political
action committees sits on the board of directors of ROAR.
Arguably, these factors may be construed to indicate control by
Senator Roberti.

Therefore, under the facts presented in this case, to ensure
that ROAR is not characterized as a controlled committee, no
elected officer or candidate, no agent of an elected officer, and
no person acting as a representative of a committee controlled by
an elected officer or candidate may sit on the board of directors
or participate in making decisions concerning the expenditures of
ROAR. (Woodruff, supra.)

However, if Shirley Wechsler is not acting on behalf of
Senator Roberti, but in her capacity as a private citizen, and if
Senator Roberti's level of involvement with ROAR is not

Our File Nos. I-90-413 and I-90-551
Page 5


significant, then Ms. Wechsler may continue to sit on Senator
Roberti's controlled committees as well as ROAR.

     Whether a candidate or elective official actually controls a
committee depends on the specific facts of each situation.  The
Legal Division of the Commission does not act as a fact-finding
body.

     I hope this letter provides you with the guidance you
requested.  If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Deanne Stone
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SH:DS:aa

Enclosures

I-90-413 and I-90-551 Dear Ms. Brainerd: You have requested advice on behalf of Respect Our Animals' Rights ("ROAR") and Sharley Wechsler, a member of ROAR's board of directors, under the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1/ Because your request is a general inquiry, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).2/ QUESTIONS 1. May a person concurrently act as treasurer for an elected state officer's political action committees and sit on the board of directors of ROAR, a political action committee formerly controlled by the same elected state officer? Does such activity create a presumption of control of ROAR by the elected state officer? 2. If the above activity is permissible, are there any restrictions as to the types of decisions in which the treasurer can participate? For example, may the treasurer participate in decisions concerning making expenditures to, or on behalf of, the elected state officer for whom she is employed? _______________________________ 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83113; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 Our File Nos. I-90-413 and I-90-551 Page 2 3. Does the additional information contained in this letter change the advice we gave in the former Brainerd Advice Letter, No. A-90-039, concerning the status of ROAR as a candidate controlled committee. CONCLUSIONS 1. and 2. If the treasurer of an elected state officer's controlled committees is a voting member of another committee's board of directors, there is a possibility that the elected officer indirectly controls the committee. If the treasurer sits on the other committee's board of directors on behalf of the elected state officer, the possibility of indirect committee control becomes a presumption. Therefore, under the facts presented in this case, to ensure that ROAR is not characterized as a committee controlled by the elected state officer, no agent of the elected state officer or person acting as a representative of a committee controlled by the state officer may sit on the board of directors or participate in making decisions concerning the expenditures of ROAR. 3. Soliciting funds on behalf of ROAR, by itself, does not indicate control of ROAR. That is why we concluded that Senator Roberti could engage in such an activity on behalf of ROAR without creating a presumption of control. Our conclusion assumed that his degree of involvement in the committee was so limited. FACTS
